item.title:
NATO SHOOTS DOWN FOURTH IRAN MISSILE OVER TURKEY
NATO intercepted another Iranian missile over Turkey on Monday, marking the fourth such incident in under a month, according to Ankara.
The missile was neutralized by air-defense systems in the Eastern Mediterranean after entering Turkish airspace.
Turkey says it has evidence the missiles originate from Iran, despite Tehran’s denials, and has warned against further escalation as NATO boosts defenses in the region.
item.description:
NATO SHOOTS DOWN FOURTH IRAN MISSILE OVER TURKEY

NATO intercepted another Iranian missile over Turkey on Monday, marking the fourth such incident in under a month, according to Ankara.

The missile was neutralized by air-defense systems in the Eastern Mediterranean after entering Turkish airspace.

Turkey says it has evidence the missiles originate from Iran, despite Tehran’s denials, and has warned against further escalation as NATO boosts defenses in the region.

item.title:
CHINESE SHIPS EXIT HORMUZ IN RARE TRANSIT
Two Cosco-linked container ships have exited the Persian Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first transit by a major Chinese operator since the war began.
The vessels — CSCL Indian Ocean and CSCL Arctic Ocean — made it through on a second attempt after turning back days earlier. Both appeared to be carrying only empty containers and signaled Chinese ownership and crew during the passage.
item.description:
CHINESE SHIPS EXIT HORMUZ IN RARE TRANSIT

Two Cosco-linked container ships have exited the Persian Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first transit by a major Chinese operator since the war began.

The vessels — CSCL Indian Ocean and CSCL Arctic Ocean — made it through on a second attempt after turning back days earlier. Both appeared to be carrying only empty containers and signaled Chinese ownership and crew during the passage.

item.description: FED'S MIRAN: TELLS CNBC, NO EVIDENCE OF WAGE-PRICE SPIRAL

item.title:
GOLDMAN: MARKETS MISREAD FED AS OIL SPIKE FUELS RATE FEARS
Goldman Sachs says investors are overestimating the chance of Fed rate hikes as oil prices surge.
Brent crude above $115 has fueled inflation fears and pushed traders to price a more hawkish Fed, but Goldman argues this reaction is overdone and out of line with history.
The bank points to 1990, when markets expected tightening after an oil shock — but the Fed ultimately cut rates as growth weakened.
Bottom line: markets are pricing higher rates, but slowing growth could force the Fed in the opposite direction.
item.description:
GOLDMAN: MARKETS MISREAD FED AS OIL SPIKE FUELS RATE FEARS

Goldman Sachs says investors are overestimating the chance of Fed rate hikes as oil prices surge.

Brent crude above $115 has fueled inflation fears and pushed traders to price a more hawkish Fed, but Goldman argues this reaction is overdone and out of line with history.

The bank points to 1990, when markets expected tightening after an oil shock — but the Fed ultimately cut rates as growth weakened.

Bottom line: markets are pricing higher rates, but slowing growth could force the Fed in the opposite direction.

item.title:
HORMUZ TRAFFIC STILL NEAR STANDSTILL
Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains far below prewar levels, despite a small number of vessels getting through.
Here’s a snapshot of traffic as of March 29:
item.description:
HORMUZ TRAFFIC STILL NEAR STANDSTILL

Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains far below prewar levels, despite a small number of vessels getting through.

Here’s a snapshot of traffic as of March 29:

item.title:
POWELL SPEAKS AT HARVARD AMID FED TURMOIL
Fed Chair Jerome Powell will speak Monday at Harvard in a rare unscripted appearance, taking questions from students at 10:30 a.m. ET.
The event comes after a turbulent period, including a now-dismissed Justice Department probe and ongoing political pressure over interest rates.
The Fed has held rates steady despite inflation near 3% and rising uncertainty from the Iran conflict, while the labor market shows signs of weakness.
With Powell’s term ending May 15 and his successor facing delays, the leadership outlook remains unclear.
item.description:
POWELL SPEAKS AT HARVARD AMID FED TURMOIL

Fed Chair Jerome Powell will speak Monday at Harvard in a rare unscripted appearance, taking questions from students at 10:30 a.m. ET.

The event comes after a turbulent period, including a now-dismissed Justice Department probe and ongoing political pressure over interest rates.

The Fed has held rates steady despite inflation near 3% and rising uncertainty from the Iran conflict, while the labor market shows signs of weakness.

With Powell’s term ending May 15 and his successor facing delays, the leadership outlook remains unclear.

item.title: RUBIO REFUSES TO SAY WHO TRUMP MEANS BY 'NEW' REGIME IN IRAN
item.description: RUBIO REFUSES TO SAY WHO TRUMP MEANS BY 'NEW' REGIME IN IRAN

item.title:
GERMAN INFLATION JUMPS ON ENERGY SHOCK
German inflation rose to 2.8% in March, the highest in over a year, as energy prices climbed 7.2% due to the Iran war.
The increase is adding pressure on the ECB to raise rates, with markets expecting a possible move as soon as April and up to three hikes this year.
Officials remain cautious, but warn higher energy costs could push inflation further if the conflict continues.
item.description:
GERMAN INFLATION JUMPS ON ENERGY SHOCK

German inflation rose to 2.8% in March, the highest in over a year, as energy prices climbed 7.2% due to the Iran war.

The increase is adding pressure on the ECB to raise rates, with markets expecting a possible move as soon as April and up to three hikes this year.

Officials remain cautious, but warn higher energy costs could push inflation further if the conflict continues.

item.title:
IRAN SETS HARD LINE ON HORMUZ TALKS
Iran’s First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref said negotiations over the Strait of Hormuz will only happen if adversaries pledge not to invade and recognize Iran’s international rights.
Echoing combative rhetoric, he added that Iran’s opponents are now “begging” for talks after the waterway was effectively shut to commercial shipping.
item.description:
IRAN SETS HARD LINE ON HORMUZ TALKS

Iran’s First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref said negotiations over the Strait of Hormuz will only happen if adversaries pledge not to invade and recognize Iran’s international rights.

Echoing combative rhetoric, he added that Iran’s opponents are now “begging” for talks after the waterway was effectively shut to commercial shipping.

item.description:
RECESSION ODDS SURGE TO 40%

Prediction markets now price a 40% chance of a recession this year — a sharp climb over recent weeks.
kalshi.com/markets/kxrecssnb…

item.title:
OIL CRISIS PUSHES BRENT TO $125
Société Générale has raised its Brent forecast, expecting prices to average $125 per barrel in April as the Strait of Hormuz disruption continues into next month.
Analysts say flows through Hormuz remain at crisis levels, leaving a roughly 15 million barrels per day gap in global supply. The region is largely stalled, with only limited rerouting by Saudi Arabia and the UAE, and occasional Iranian shipments.
item.description:
OIL CRISIS PUSHES BRENT TO $125

Société Générale has raised its Brent forecast, expecting prices to average $125 per barrel in April as the Strait of Hormuz disruption continues into next month.

Analysts say flows through Hormuz remain at crisis levels, leaving a roughly 15 million barrels per day gap in global supply. The region is largely stalled, with only limited rerouting by Saudi Arabia and the UAE, and occasional Iranian shipments.
